verses.text: The Ziphites betray David to Saul once more, and Saul comes again with three thousand men. David scouts the camp himself. The same betrayal, the same hunt — but David does not grow careless; he watches, waits, and looks to see what God will do.

The Ziphites betray David to Saul once more, and Saul comes again with three thousand men. David scouts the camp himself. The same betrayal, the same hunt — but David does not grow careless; he watches, waits, and looks to see what God will do.

By night David and Abishai slip into the very midst of the sleeping camp, right to where Saul lies. Abishai whispers, 'God hath delivered thine enemy... let me smite him.' The chance looks heaven-sent. But an opportunity to do wrong is not the same as God's permission.

By night David and Abishai slip into the very midst of the sleeping camp, right to where Saul lies. Abishai whispers, 'God hath delivered thine enemy... let me smite him.' The chance looks heaven-sent. But an opportunity to do wrong is not the same as God's permission.

By night David and Abishai slip into the very midst of the sleeping camp, right to where Saul lies. Abishai whispers, 'God hath delivered thine enemy... let me smite him.' The chance looks heaven-sent. But an opportunity to do wrong is not the same as God's permission.

David refuses: 'who can stretch forth his hand against the LORD's anointed, and be guiltless?' He will let God deal with Saul — by death, or time, or battle — but he will not lift his own hand. Reverence for what God has set apart restrains him from sin.

David refuses: 'who can stretch forth his hand against the LORD's anointed, and be guiltless?' He will let God deal with Saul — by death, or time, or battle — but he will not lift his own hand. Reverence for what God has set apart restrains him from sin.

David refuses: 'who can stretch forth his hand against the LORD's anointed, and be guiltless?' He will let God deal with Saul — by death, or time, or battle — but he will not lift his own hand. Reverence for what God has set apart restrains him from sin.

They take only Saul's spear and water cruse and slip away, for 'a deep sleep from the LORD' had fallen on the camp. God Himself made the rescue possible, holding the whole army in slumber. When He means to deliver, even an armed camp cannot stir.

From a safe distance David calls out, mocking Abner for failing to guard the king — proof of how near death had come. He exposes the danger without ever causing it. A clear conscience can speak boldly, having done no wrong it needs to hide.

Saul knows David's voice, and David pleads: 'why doth my lord pursue his servant? what have I done?' He asks only to be left alone to serve God. The innocent may rightly protest their treatment, yet David does it humbly, calling himself a flea, a hunted partridge.

Saul knows David's voice, and David pleads: 'why doth my lord pursue his servant? what have I done?' He asks only to be left alone to serve God. The innocent may rightly protest their treatment, yet David does it humbly, calling himself a flea, a hunted partridge.

Saul confesses, 'I have sinned... I have played the fool,' and blesses David. David sends back the spear, refusing to keep even a trophy of his mercy, and trusts the LORD to reward righteousness. They part for the last time — Saul softened for an hour, David steadfast to the end.
